# ⚠️ Current Weaknesses & Research Upgrade Plan

> **Project:** Detection of Abnormal Behavior in Linux Servers Using Machine Learning
>
> **Purpose:** This document identifies the gaps in our current implementation that prevent it from being research-grade, and proposes specific fixes for each.

---

## 🔴 Current Weaknesses

### 1. Synthetic Data Only — No Real-World Validation

**Problem:** All 2000 log entries are generated by our own script (`generate_sample_logs.py`). The patterns are clean and well-separated, which makes ML models appear more effective than they would be on real data.

**Why it matters for research:**
- Reviewers will immediately question: *"Does this work on real servers?"*
- Decision Tree achieving 100% accuracy is a red flag — suggests labels are too simple
- No proof of generalization

**Fix:**
- Download **real public log datasets** from [LogHub](https://github.com/logpai/loghub):
  - **HDFS dataset** — 11M+ log lines from Hadoop Distributed File System
  - **BGL dataset** — Blue Gene/L supercomputer logs with labeled anomalies
  - **Linux dataset** — Real Linux syslog data
- Run the **same pipeline** on both synthetic and real data
- Compare results side by side

---

### 2. Decision Tree Gets 100% — Likely Too Simple Labels

**Problem:** The Decision Tree learned a single rule: `IF breakin_attempts > 0 → abnormal`. This is because our labeling heuristic in `feature_extractor.py` uses the same condition. The model is essentially learning our rules back.

**Why it matters for research:**
- 100% accuracy in anomaly detection is unrealistic
- Suggests **data leakage** — the labeling logic is too similar to the features
- Real anomaly detection papers report 85–95% F1-scores

**Fix:**
- Create **multi-class labels** instead of binary:
  - `normal`, `brute_force`, `credential_stuffing`, `privilege_escalation`, `breakin`
- Use **external labels** from real datasets (HDFS has human-annotated labels)
- Add **noise** to labels to simulate real-world label uncertainty
- Implement **semi-supervised learning** — train on partially labeled data

---

### 3. No Cross-Validation — Single Train/Test Split

**Problem:** We use a single 80/20 split with `random_state=42`. Results depend heavily on which samples end up in train vs. test.

**Why it matters for research:**
- Not statistically rigorous
- Cannot compute confidence intervals
- Results may not be reproducible with different random seeds

**Fix:**
- Implement **Stratified K-Fold Cross Validation** (k=5 or k=10)
- Report **mean ± standard deviation** for all metrics
- Run experiments with **multiple random seeds** (42, 123, 456, 789, 0)

---

### 4. No Hyperparameter Tuning

**Problem:** All models use default or manually chosen parameters. We didn't systematically search for the best configuration.

**Why it matters for research:**
- Cannot claim models are performing at their best
- No evidence that parameters were optimized
- Reviewers expect grid search or Bayesian optimization

**Fix:**
- Implement **GridSearchCV** for each model:
  - Decision Tree: `max_depth` [3, 5, 7, 10], `min_samples_split` [2, 5, 10]
  - Isolation Forest: `n_estimators` [50, 100, 200], `contamination` [0.1, 0.15, 0.2, 0.25]
  - PCA: `n_components` [0.85, 0.90, 0.95, 0.99], threshold percentile [90, 95, 99]
- Report **best parameters found** for each model
- Compare default vs. tuned performance

---

### 5. Only 4 Basic Models — Missing Modern Approaches

**Problem:** We only test Rule-Based, Isolation Forest, PCA, and Decision Tree. These are all relatively simple.

**Why it matters for research:**
- Missing commonly cited models in anomaly detection literature
- No ensemble methods
- No deep learning baseline

**Fix — Add these models:**

| Model | Type | Why Include |
|-------|------|------------|
| **Random Forest** | Supervised Ensemble | More robust than single Decision Tree, reduces overfitting |
| **XGBoost** | Gradient Boosting | State-of-the-art for tabular data, widely used in Kaggle |
| **One-Class SVM** | Unsupervised | Classic anomaly detection, good for comparison |
| **MLP Neural Network** | Supervised Deep Learning | Shows we explored neural approaches |
| **Autoencoder** | Unsupervised Deep Learning | Modern anomaly detection via reconstruction error |

---

### 6. No Literature Review — No Context In Research Landscape

**Problem:** We don't reference any published papers or compare our results against known benchmarks.

**Why it matters for research:**
- Cannot position our work relative to existing research
- No evidence of awareness of prior art
- Reviewers expect a "Related Work" section

**Fix:**
- Add a **Related Work** section referencing key papers:
  - He et al. (2016) — "Experience Report: System Log Analysis for Anomaly Detection"
  - Liu et al. (2008) — "Isolation Forest" (original paper)
  - Du et al. (2017) — "DeepLog: Anomaly Detection and Diagnosis from System Logs through Deep Learning"
  - Xu et al. (2009) — "Detecting Large-Scale System Problems by Mining Console Logs"
- Compare our F1-scores against published benchmarks
- Write a formal **Literature Survey** section

---

### 7. No Ablation Study — Don't Know What Actually Matters

**Problem:** We have 15 features but don't know which ones are critical. The Decision Tree says only `breakin_attempts` matters, but this may not hold for other models or real data.

**Why it matters for research:**
- Cannot justify feature selection
- No understanding of feature contribution
- Research papers always include ablation studies

**Fix:**
- Systematically **remove one feature at a time** and measure F1 drop
- Group features into categories and test category-level ablation:
  - Event counts only
  - Ratio features only
  - Time-based features only
  - All combined
- Plot **feature ablation heatmap**

---

### 8. No Statistical Significance Testing

**Problem:** We compare models by raw F1-scores without testing if differences are statistically significant.

**Why it matters for research:**
- A 2% difference might be random noise, not a real improvement
- Reviewers expect p-values or confidence intervals

**Fix:**
- Run **paired t-tests** between model pairs across K-fold splits
- Report **95% confidence intervals** for each metric
- Use **McNemar's test** for comparing classifiers on the same test set

---

## 🟢 Implementation Priority

| Priority | Fix | Impact | Effort |
|----------|-----|--------|--------|
| **P0 — Critical** | Real dataset (HDFS/BGL) | 🔴🔴🔴 Highest | Medium |
| **P0 — Critical** | K-Fold Cross Validation | 🔴🔴🔴 Highest | Low |
| **P1 — High** | More models (RF, XGBoost, SVM) | 🟠🟠 High | Medium |
| **P1 — High** | Hyperparameter tuning | 🟠🟠 High | Medium |
| **P1 — High** | Literature review / Related Work | 🟠🟠 High | Low |
| **P2 — Medium** | Ablation study | 🟡 Medium | Low |
| **P2 — Medium** | Multi-class labels | 🟡 Medium | Medium |
| **P3 — Nice to have** | Statistical significance tests | 🟢 Low-Med | Low |
| **P3 — Nice to have** | Autoencoder / Deep Learning | 🟢 Low-Med | High |

---

## 📅 Proposed Phase 5 — Research Enhancement

### Phase 5A: Data & Methodology (Week 10)
- [ ] Download HDFS dataset from LogHub
- [ ] Adapt parser for HDFS log format
- [ ] Implement K-Fold Cross Validation (k=10)
- [ ] Add multi-class labeling

### Phase 5B: Model Expansion (Week 11)
- [ ] Implement Random Forest
- [ ] Implement XGBoost (via `xgboost` library)
- [ ] Implement One-Class SVM
- [ ] Implement MLP Neural Network
- [ ] Hyperparameter tuning with GridSearchCV

### Phase 5C: Analysis & Writing (Week 12)
- [ ] Run ablation study
- [ ] Statistical significance testing
- [ ] Write Literature Review section
- [ ] Write IEEE-format research paper
- [ ] Prepare final presentation

## 📊 Expected Improvement

| Metric | Current (Synthetic) | Expected (Real Data + Enhancements) |
|--------|-------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Decision Tree F1 | 1.00 (suspicious) | 0.85–0.95 (realistic) |
| Best Model F1 | 1.00 | 0.90–0.95 |
| Models compared | 4 | 8–9 |
| Validation method | Single split | 10-fold CV with confidence intervals |
| Data source | Synthetic only | Synthetic + real (HDFS) |
| Research paper | None | IEEE-format, 8–10 pages |
